summary: LogicSAGE is a neuro-symbolic framework for LLM logical reasoning that couples a fine-tuned neural reasoner (System 1) with a symbolic solver (System 2) through a dual-mode "Socratic" error-correction agent. In its reactive mode, the agent treats solver error messages as pedagogical feedback and iteratively repairs invalid logic programs rather than discarding them. In its proactive mode, it monitors token-level entropy during program generation, separating syntactic and semantic token streams and computing a normalized (Z-score) entropy score to flag translations that execute successfully but are semantically wrong (silent failures) that solvers cannot detect. An adaptive fusion module reconciles the symbolic and neural outputs, falling back to neural reasoning when formalization fails. Built on a fine-tuned Qwen3-8B trained on solver-verified traces distilled from a larger model, the system is evaluated on five logical reasoning benchmarks (PrOntoQA, ProofWriter, FOLIO, LogicalDeduction, AR-LSAT), where it reports 92.36% average accuracy, outperforming GPT-4-based baselines and prior neuro-symbolic frameworks such as Logic-LM and VERUS-LM. The paper supports these claims with ablations decomposing the reactive and proactive components, a comparison of the dual-process design against its System-1-only and System-2-only variants, and a latency-accuracy trade-off analysis.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper tackles a real and well-motivated problem, the brittleness of neural-to-symbolic translation in solver-in-the-loop reasoning, and its most distinctive idea, proactive uncertainty probing, is genuinely original. Separating the generated token stream into syntactic and semantic components and computing an intra-stream, Z-score-normalized entropy score to flag semantically wrong but syntactically valid translations is a principled and, to my knowledge, novel mechanism, and isolating "silent semantic failure" as a first-class problem is a useful conceptual framing. On soundness, the empirical study is broad: five established benchmarks spanning deductive reasoning, first-order logic, constraint satisfaction, and analytical reasoning; well-decomposed ablations that separate the reactive and proactive components and contrast System-1-only, System-2-only, and the full model; and a latency-accuracy trade-off analysis that yields concrete deployment guidance. Presentation is generally strong: the manuscript is readable and well organized, the method and motivation figures are high quality, and the appendix (prompt templates, runtime setup, confidence distributions) aids reproducibility.

That said, several issues temper my assessment. The most serious is that the headline gains are confounded and cannot be cleanly attributed to the proposed architecture. The reported baselines use different and generally older or weaker backbones (Logic-LM and Logic-LM++ on GPT-4, VERUS-LM on an unspecified backbone), while LogicSAGE uses a fine-tuned Qwen3-8B; the paper reports no standalone Qwen3-8B or Qwen3-8B+CoT numbers on the same tasks, and external evidence suggests this backbone is already highly competitive on these benchmarks. Compounding this, the system depends on SFT over solver-verified traces distilled from a much larger model: the base model reaches only 80.30% (below VERUS-LM's 85.02%), and the jump to 92.36% arrives with SFT, yet no experiment compares an SFT model run without the Socratic loop at inference time. As a result, the reader cannot tell how much of the improvement comes from the Socratic architecture versus from a stronger backbone and higher-quality training data.

A second concern is that the paper's emphasis does not match where the empirical gains originate. The ablations indicate that reactive, syntactic error correction is responsible for the large majority of the improvement, whereas proactive semantic probing, the paper's most novel component, contributes only a small increment on most datasets and is materially helpful mainly on AR-LSAT. The paper's value is therefore better understood as a well-engineered systems contribution than as a result anchored on the entropy mechanism, and the writing would be more convincing if it explicitly characterized the problem classes for which the dual approach is actually necessary. Relatedly, the validation of the entropy mechanism is thin: it is assessed only through aggregate accuracy, with no precision/recall for spike detection, no accounting of confident-but-wrong translations (low entropy, wrong semantics) that the mechanism cannot see, and no sensitivity analysis for the NES > alpha threshold, so the claim that probing "resolves" silent failures is not fully substantiated.

On originality and framing, while the entropy idea is novel, the surrounding contribution is more incremental than the paper suggests. The "Generate-and-Hope" characterization of prior work reads as a strawman given that Logic-LM already incorporates solver-error self-correction, Logic-LM++ performs multi-step refinement, and iterative repair from compiler/solver feedback is standard in code generation and in prior neuro-symbolic work (e.g., LLM-ARC). The "architectural innovation can supersede model scale" claim is also over-reached: the comparison is against GPT-4, a 2023-era non-reasoning model, rather than reasoning-specialized systems that define the current state of the art. Some phrasing is imprecise or overstated, for instance attributing semantic misinterpretation to the solver or the "program" when the error really lies in the NL-to-logic translation step, and claiming the framework "resolves" translation fragility. Finally, the evaluation rests on a single backbone at one scale (8B), leaving generalization open; Figure 2 is overloaded and hard to follow; the fusion policy is underspecified (statistics on how often each branch is trusted, and whether System 1 is genuinely selected on AR-LSAT despite its weak standalone accuracy, would help); and there are minor citation-spacing and punctuation errors. On significance, the contribution is practically useful but appears most relevant to the specialized class of solver-in-the-loop reasoning systems, which bounds its broader impact.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Can you provide standalone Qwen3-8B (direct-answer) and Qwen3-8B+CoT baselines on all five benchmarks, and ideally run at least one existing baseline (e.g., Logic-LM or VERUS-LM) with the same Qwen3-8B backbone, so that the architectural contribution can be separated from the strength of the underlying backbone?

2. Can you add an ablation of the SFT model without the Socratic loop at inference time (single-pass SFT generation + solver execution) versus the full LogicSAGE pipeline, to disentangle the contribution of the Socratic architecture from the SFT and solver-verified data quality? This is the result most likely to affect my evaluation.

3. Can you report precision and recall for entropy-spike detection (of all flagged spikes, how many were real semantic errors; of all real semantic errors, how many produced a spike), the rate of confident-but-wrong translations invisible to the mechanism, and the sensitivity of results to the NES > alpha threshold?

4. In which classes of examples is proactive semantic probing essential, given that reactive correction drives most of the gain and probing helps mainly on AR-LSAT? Relatedly, what is the distribution of final outputs across the fusion branches (Eq. 4), and is System 1 actually selected and helpful on AR-LSAT despite its weak standalone performance?

5. Can you provide a cost/compute analysis (how often refinement is triggered, typical number of rounds, and per-component and overall latency) with test-time-compute-matched comparisons, and does the "supersede model scale" claim still hold against reasoning-specialized models (e.g., o1, o3-mini, DeepSeek-R1)?
limitations: Partially. The paper discusses inference latency and outlines future directions, but the limitations discussion is incomplete. It does not acknowledge that the current experimental design cannot disentangle the gains from SFT and solver-verified supervision versus the Socratic architecture, nor the failure modes of entropy-based detection (notably confident-but-wrong translations), and it omits the absence of comparisons against reasoning-specialized models despite the claim of superseding model scale. A note on generalization beyond a single 8B backbone and on code availability would further strengthen it.
